entry.title: Streamlining Value Mapping Management in SAP CI: Exporting to CSV and Handling Large Datasets
entry.content:
Introduction:

Value mappings are essential for data transformation in SAP Cloud Integration (CI). They allow you to define correspondences between different data representations, ensuring seamless integration across systems. However, managing and transferring value mappings can sometimes present challenges, especially when dealing with large datasets or when needing to work with the data outside of the CPI environment. In this blog post, I'll share my experience in overcoming these hurdles by exporting value mappings to CSV format and handling large record counts.

The Challenge: Exporting and Importing Value Mappings as CSV

While SAP CPI provides tools to manage value mappings within the platform, there are scenarios where exporting them to CSV format becomes crucial. For example:

  • External Data Analysis: You might need to analyze or manipulate the value mapping data using external tools like spreadsheets or databases.
  • Mass Data Migration: When migrating or replicating value mappings across different CPI tenants, a CSV format simplifies the process.
  • Backup and Version Control: CSV exports offer a convenient way to back up value mappings and integrate them into version control systems.

Unfortunately, CPI doesn't offer a direct "Export to CSV" feature for value mappings.

The Solution: Leveraging Groovy Scripting for XML to CSV Conversion

To address this, I developed a Groovy script that converts the XML representation of a value mapping (extracted from the CPI artifact) into CSV format. Here's the process:

  1. Exporting the Value Mapping:

    • In the CPI Web UI, export the required value mapping as an archive (.zip file).

 

                    

    • Unzip the archive.
    • Locate the XML file containing the value mapping data.

  2. Groovy Script Conversion:

    • The following Groovy script parses the XML file and generates CSV output:
    Groovy
     
    import groovy.xml.XmlSlurper
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.ArrayList;

def xmlInput = new File("your_value_mapping.xml").text // Replace with your XML file name
def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(xmlInput)
def groups = xml.group
def data = [:]

groups.each { group ->
    def iflowName = group.entry.find { it.agency.text() == 'CPI_FLOW' && it.schema.text() == 'iflow_name' }?.value.text()
    group.entry.each { entry ->
        if (entry.agency.text() == 'CRIBL') {
            def schema = entry.schema.text()
            def value = entry.value.text()
            if (!data.containsKey(schema)) {
                data[schema] = []
            }
            data[schema].add([iflowName, value])
        }
    }
}

data.each { schema, values ->
    def fileName = "${schema.toLowerCase()}.csv"
    def header = ",CPI_FLOW|iflow_name,CRIBL|${schema}"
    def csvContent = new StringBuilder(header + "\n")
    values.eachWithIndex { row, index ->
        csvContent.append("${index + 1},${row[0]},${row[1]}\n")
    }
    new File(fileName).text = csvContent.toString()
    println "Created file: ${fileName}"
}

def file = new File("ci.csv");
def content = new StringBuilder(",CPI_FLOW|iflow_name,CRIBL|CI\n");
int index = 1;
groups.each{group->
    def iflowName = group.entry.find { it.agency.text() == 'CPI_FLOW' && it.schema.text() == 'iflow_name' }?.value.text()
    group.entry.each { entry ->
        if (entry.agency.text() == 'CRIBL' && entry.schema.text() == 'CI') {
            def value = entry.value.text();
            content.append("${index},${iflowName},${value}\n");
            index++;
        }
    }
}

file.text = content.toString();
println "Created file: ci.csv";
    • Replace "your_value_mapping.xml" with the actual filename.
    • Replace agency and identifier with your actual names 
    • Run the script using a Groovy interpreter or in iflow
    • You can modify the groovy script with your specific requirement as in my case I used the groovy in an iflow providing the xml in input and get the respective VM csv data

                  

 

 

 

  1. Importing the CSV:

    • The generated CSV files can then be imported into another value mapping artifact.

Handling Large Datasets: Breaking Down the CSV

I encountered an issue when dealing with value mappings containing more than 300 records. Attempting to import a large CSV file resulted in errors. To resolve this, I recommend the following:

  • Splitting the CSV: Divide the generated CSV file into smaller chunks, each containing a manageable number of records (e.g., 200-300).
  • Partial Imports: Import each CSV chunk separately into the target value mapping.

Conclusion:

By using Groovy scripting to convert value mapping XML to CSV, you can overcome the limitations of direct CSV export in SAP CPI. This approach simplifies data analysis, migration, and backup tasks. When working with large datasets, remember to split the CSV files to ensure successful imports.

I hope this blog post provides valuable insights for managing value mappings in SAP CPI. Feel free to share your experiences and questions in the comments below!

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite – Connect Everything FEATURING WORKDAY
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog is the second part of our “Connect Everything” campaign. If you haven’t heard about it yet, check out the main blog post series for an overview of our campaign.

What does this blog cover?

In this post, we will demonstrate how easily a Workday solution can be integrated with SAP S/4HANA  Cloud. This seamless connection is made possible by the SAP Integration Suite, which ensures smooth and efficient integration between the two systems.

Integration Scenario

Many organizations use Workday as their primary HR system for managing employee data, while SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is used for enterprise operations. To ensure accurate HR and system access management, companies need a reliable integration that automatically replicates employee records from Workday to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, keeping workforce data consistent across systems.

With this integration, employees from Workday are created as Workforce Persons in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. At the same time, this process enables automatic user provisioning, meaning that once an employee is replicated, the corresponding business user is also created in SAP, granting them access to relevant applications and processes.

As shown in the process figure below, a scheduled job in SAP Cloud Integration queries Workday for any updated or newly created employee records. Workday processes the request and returns the relevant employee data. If no changes are found, the process ends. Otherwise, the data is transformed into the required SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition format. The transformed records are then sent to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, where employees are either created or updated as Workforce Persons, ensuring their details are up to date.

 

How to set up the scenario?

Overview:

  1. Find the right scenario
  2. Prepare SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  3. Prepare Workday
  4. Configure Cloud Integration

 

1.Find the right scenario at API Business Hub

SAP offers a wide range of pre-built integration content for Workday and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. To explore what’s available, visit the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, where you can filter by the systems you need and find ready-to-use integration packages that streamline the setup process.

  • Open SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • Search for the package SAP S/4HANA Cloud Integration with Workday
  • Select Replicate Employee from Workday to SAP S4HANA Cloud

 

  • Here, you can discover and analyze the integration flow
  • Under Documents, you will find the link to the documentation, which you can read through


2.Prepare SAP S/4HANA Cloud

  • Create the Communication System and Communication User
    • Create new User for Inbound Communication
    • Save your credentials for cloud integration configuration

Communication System

Communication User

 

  • Create Communication Arrangement
  • Choose Scenario -> SAP_COM_0301
  • Maintain your created communication system and Inbound User 
  • Save the SOAP API Endpoint Replicate Basic Master Data for Workforce from External HR Systems for cloud integration configuration

Communication Arrangement

3.Prepare Workday

Check instruction in the documentation of the Integration Flow -> Configuration Guide

4.Configure Cloud Integration

  • As a prerequisite, first install the Workday Adapter from the discover menu for prepackaged integration content  in your environment
  • Once successfully copied to your design runtime, deploy the Workday Adapter.
  • After you install the Workday Adapter, copy the package ->SAP S/4HANA Cloud Integration with Workday to your own design runtime

Workday Package

  • Select the value mapping artifact
  • Maintain your value mapping for the GenderCode and CompanyCode, as required in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Select the Replicate Employee from Workday to SAP S4HANA Cloud  Integration Flow and click on configure

Artifacts

  • Here maintain your Workday tenant configuration values like tenant url, credentials 
  • Maintain the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition parameters, which were set up in the previous step.
    • Configure the inbound endpoint of SAP S/4HANA Cloud 
    • Maintain also SAP S/4HANA Cloud credentials for authentication.

Conclusion 

Setting up the Workday to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Integration was easier than expected, thanks to prebuilt content available in the SAP Integration Suite. Instead of starting from scratch, we could leverage ready-to-use integration flows that only required minimal configuration. However, for businesses with more complex needs, there’s always the option to customize or build entirely new integrations

Have you worked on a Workday-SAP integration before? What were your key takeaways? Let’s start a discussion—share your insights or drop your questions in the comments. And stay tuned for more content in our "Connect Everything" series!

entry.title: Dynamic Interface on Cloud Integration - part 4
entry.content:
I’ll break this article into:

  • Problem/Context 
  • Our approach 
  • Git into Partner Directory 
  • Generic iflow (this link)
  • Automated unit testing
  • Limitations and Future plans

As with any interface, this generic iflow is split in three parts:

  1. The receiver protocol
  2. Transformations
  3. The sender

Receiver

To receive the data from our consumers we support AS2, HTTP, AMQP adapter, AdvancedEventMesh adapter. I'll not show here all details but lets go through some of the most interesting parts.

If you remember from part 2, the url for our scenario was:

https://<cloudintegrationrturl>/http/TestpackageNUPE/FER_DynamicInterfaceTransformAndRoute/NUPEBPAPOC

By configuring our receive as above (with the * wildcard), we allow any interfaceid to come as part of the request. How do intercept which interface id have we received? Using a content modifier as below

Transformations

With this key id, we have everything we need not to check which configuration exists for this on partner directory. This can be achieved by querying the Partner Directory API as done below:

 

 

def service = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService. class , null);
    if (service == null) {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Partner Directory Service not found");
    }
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message);
    if (messageLog != null) {
        messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("FER_InterfaceID", interfaceId);
    }
    try {
        def lookupRules = service.getParameter("LookupRules", namespace + interfaceId, String. class);
        if (lookupRules != null) {
            def decodedBytes = Base64.getDecoder().decode(lookupRules)
            String lookupRulesText = new String(decodedBytes, "UTF-8");
            logMessage <<= "Found lookup rules [${lookupRulesText}] for partner id [${namespace+interfaceId}]\n";

 

 

Then we parse the lookupRules file loaded and we iterate all rules there one by one. I'll save you from those details, it's just groovy code. 

After complete iteration we'll have the final parameter set and values and the payload was transformed already according to the respective rules.

If something goes wrong (an exception is thrown), or if we want to see the details of all lookups and transformations and intermediate payloads we can enable debug or trace into the iflow. In any of these cases we'll then dump a TraceLog file into the MPL as attachment so that it can be checked later on by our support team.

 

 

String logLevel = message.getProperty('SAP_MessageProcessingLogConfiguration').logLevel.toString()
        if (logLevel in ['DEBUG', 'TRACE'] || (traceLog!=null && !"".equals(traceLog))) {
            logMessageAsAttachment = true;
        }
    } catch (Exception ex) {
        logMessageAsAttachment = true;
        throw ex;
    } finally {
        if (logMessageAsAttachment && isLoggingOnErrorEnabled) {
            String logMessageStr = logMessage.toString()
            message.setProperty("log", logMessageStr)
            messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)?.addAttachmentAsString('TraceLog', logMessageStr, 'text/plain')
        }
    }

 

 

Sender

Now we can optionally have a process direct step (depending on the configuration files defined) pointing into a custom iflow

This can be very handy whenever the target requires a non-standard delivery approach (fetching special tokens or nounces, etc). If that's the case we can still leverage all transformations done before and use the process direct just to develop the delivery part.

If the target is quite standard, it will likely fall into something our generic iflow already supports. For sending the data we support the following adapters: HTTP, IDOC, AdvancedEventMesh, AMQP and SFTP. Others can be added later if needed but for now, we had no need to enhance it further.

In this chapter we covered everything from "runtime" perspective, meaning picking the configurations from partner directory and executing according to the payload that was supplied. On next chapter we'll talk about unit testing

As usual, feel free to provide ideas or suggestions based on your experience

entry.title: Overcoming SAP CPI Monitoring Limitations: Retrieving Attachments via OData API
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) provides robust monitoring capabilities through its Message Processing Logs (MPL), where users can track message processing and retrieve attachments. However, a common challenge arises when the SAP CPI monitoring UI limits attachment visibility to only the first 500 attachments, even when there are thousands.

This blog post provides a detailed guide on how to bypass this limitation and retrieve all attachments using the SAP CPI OData API.

Retrieving Attachments via SAP CPI OData API

          1.Identify Your CPI Tenant URL

For Neo Environment:

https://<tenant Id>-tmn.hci.<landscape>.hana.ondemand.com/

For Cloud Foundry Environment:

https://<tenant Id>.<system id, e.g. it-cpi001>.cfapps.<landscape>.hana.ondemand.com/

          2.Authenticate Your API Request

SAP CPI API requests require Basic Authentication or OAuth 2.0.

Using Basic Authentication, include your credentials as a base64-encoded username and password. Basic Authentication Headers:

Authorization: Basic <base64-encoded-username:password>

Accept: application/json

Or, if using Postman, you can put the username and password directly in the Authorization

If your tenant enforces OAuth 2.0, you first need to obtain an access token and use it:

Authorization: Bearer <your-access-token>

Accept: application/json

          3.Retrieve All Attachments

Use the following API request to fetch all attachments related to a specific message ID:

GET https://<your-cpi-tenant>/api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs('<messageId>')/Attachments

The Message ID can be found in the SAP CPI Monitor section:

          4.View the Content of a Specific Attachment

Once you retrieve the list of attachments, each record will have an <id> field. You can use this id to download the attachment content by appending /$value to the request.

Example API Request to Download an Attachment

GET

https://<your-cpi-tenant>:<port(443)>/api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs('<messageId>')/Attachments('<attachmentId>')/$value

Conclusion

While SAP CPI’s monitoring UI limits attachment visibility to the first 500 records, leveraging API capabilities allows you to retrieve and manage attachments beyond this restriction.

entry.title: Blog series on my CPI + Camel learning journey - Part1 - The tool
entry.content:
We start our series with a proper post about the tool I am building to assist me in my learning.
Hopefully, it can become something useful for wider audience.

Link back to index: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/blog-series-on-my-cpi-camel-learning-journey/ba-p/14031053

About arhitecture

First of all, it's worth mentioning that I probably would not have developed this thing without sap community blogs, specifically this one by @vadimklimov  and that one by @ArielBravo 

These gentlemen definitely can be called godfathers of capić, and I do hope they don't mind me including their scripts into my project.

But it all started with git of course, because as I mentioned, I am not a fan of low-code approach, therefore after I learned that under the hood we do have real camel, I started looking for a way to deal with routes as proper code.

So at first there was just a way to browse remote stuff via odata api and manually download bundles, as of course I discovered Operations endpoint (first at tenant level where sap ui allows you to download bundles via it).

Btw this blog  also by Ariel regarding Operations service is worth mentioning as maybe we'll implement more features it supports later (why not after all, as we already have a cap client service for it).

So, finally I discovered those posts I mentioned earlier, and also understood that Operations endpoint was also available at runtime instance level (meaning it is reachable the same way).

And in the it felt like we could combine all those pieces into something interesting.

With that let's take a look at our architecture in terms of services we consume and clients we implement.

Integration flow runtime service instance params are default

 

{
  "grant-types": [
    "client_credentials"
  ],
  "redirect-uris": [],
  "token-validity": 3600,
  "roles": [
    "ESBMessaging.send"
  ]
}

 

Api runtime service instance params (rezip requires WorkspacePackagesEdit iirc)

 

{
  "grant-types": [
    "client_credentials"
  ],
  "redirect-uris": [],
  "token-validity": 43200,
  "roles": [
    "WorkspacePackagesRead",
    "MonitoringArtifactsDeploy",
    "MonitoringDataRead",
    "WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy",
    "WorkspaceArtifactLocksRead",
    "CatalogPackageArtifactsRead",
    "WorkspacePackagesEdit",
    "WorkspacePackagesConfigure"
  ]
}

 

Here coloured ovals are CAP services I modelled as clients for remote endpoints.

Green would mean that api/usage is public (and provided by sap) and rather conventional:

  •  https://api.sap.com/api/IntegrationContent/resource is public odata api,
  • and iflows service is just a convenience wrapper (as we already have creds) to be able to call/test deployed artifacts (we have some kind of rudimentary http client to send some data from ui)

Yellow Operations, already mentioned above - is kinda available (and also provided by sap), but not documented or recommended (though cpi ui is basically built on top of it)

Orange - those are our illegal magic iflows we deployed into cpi to be able to extract information and files.

Our app also exposes vanilla express endpoints via express-http-proxy (those 3 tiles leading to localhost /cpi) which uses basic auth from iflows creds.

Persistence

What that means in terms of cap app architecture? Well, mostly that we are really lightweight and just act as proxy for remote services and local file system.

Speaking of file system - we are transient in terms of cap persistence. All of our entities are modelled as  @cds.persistence.skip (so currently we don't even use in-memory sqlite)

What that means is except for two standard files .env (for config) and .cdsrc.json (for creds) there is NO state inside capic folder.

Lets take a look at that now:

Ok, so what we have here is again, in grey our config properties and corresponding entities:

  • CPI_TENANT_URL - is just a link to tenant for navigation to content (from packages ui, of course)
  • CPI_EXPORT_PATH - is a dir where we store our content (by defaul points to samples to rezip webshell to bootstrap all the stuff, later must point to proper dir, eg ../cpi ). Each package is a separate repo, each sync action is a commit (if there were changes). Also for now we put edited blueprints we tweak to run on our local karaf as blueprint-local.xml.
  • FTP_DIR - is an ftp root for our embedded ftp server we spin from server.js. Serves obvious reason to let us communicate to our camel routes via ftp ui.
  • finally, KARAF_PATH is our karaf installation where we deploy blueprints to. There's also hidden JAR_DIR property to point to folder where our setup script would pull jars to from remote via our webshell (file browser part by Ariel to be specific).

Well, that's basically it, pretty simple.

Again, in a nutshell it is just a small sap cap application with some vanilla nodejs/express modules and freestyle ui5 on top of fiori elements.

About project setup and intended workflow.

Now, let's take a look at intended setup and workflow. Of course, it's just my gut feeling about it from both user and product owner perspectives, but I feel like the direction or vision is more or less clear.

Okay, as we basically just want to sync content from cpi (I only refer to non-PROD instance of course, where we develop our stuff), and our cap app points only to one CPI_TENANT_URL it makes sense to assume the following setup:

I'd say that looks rather trivial.

Color coding in this case means how much we care about data in particular container (folder).

It is rather obvious that there are tools like flashpipe that allow to sync cpi and remote git repo, so we are not aiming to cover CI/CD here (as I am new here, I am most likely unaware of other tools).

Obviously, nothing prevents you from having other folders like "docs" or "specs" in project folder, but it's not shown on diagram for now.

So, except for git we only care about FTP data, because there currently it is where our test data lives.

Basically, folder structure is simple and looks as follows (for now at least):

  • Each bundle/iflow is a folder in ftp root, eg "Basics_Sender_Initiated_External".
  • Subfolders in / out / in_cache are created via ftp ui (user action)
    • in for now has only DATA_IN file for current message
    • in_cache stores all input files we created
    • out serves as temporary storage for results (cleared manually)
    • most likely we will also need folders for errors and out_cache, idk..

As bundle names are unique there can only be one artifact with certain id per tenant (packages do not exist at runtime), we are fine with that approach.

What about local development?

Another  thing to mention here is that on a diagram we have IDE, where developer most likely tracks changes via git plugins, BUT also there we can do real local development.

I guess, there is a way to do proper development of Groovy scripts and at least we can develop and test XSLT mappings (I'll include an example of XSLT transformation from basics guidelines in next post).

Now the good question would be: can we use the tool or local ide and karaf to develop or at least to draft our iflows?

I would say, yes, to some extent, because if our iflow is properly modularized, we can just tinker with blueprint, and then model it on CPI (though technically I guess there might be a way to deploy edited bundle with basically just camel blueprint in it back via Operations endpoint or another webshell script).

And I feel like this could be the direction for a tool: some kind of hybrid approach for pro-code developers.

Dev workflow

Ok, having said that, I feel like the workflow can look like this:

  1. Prepare project dir and cpi (create service instances and keys)
  2. clone capić tool and do the setup (make sure you run on *nix or wsl for windows)
  3. Rezip (deploy) webshell flows and scripts to cpi (strictly speaking, only needed to pull jars for karaf, so you can do this on trial account)
  4. Download karaf and run setup to have it running (again, jars dir can be copied from another place)
  5. Start/continue developing content on CPI side and syncing it to local git and then to local karaf
  6. Play with blueprints locally, mock external systems data, test/debug via ftp ui etc
  7. Maybe write/adjust automated tests for your local flows
  8. Push it all somewhere to remote git (most likely separate repos for data and actual artifacts) so that later it could be pulled for some kind of automated testing (I don't have anything implemented yet)
  9. Sync changes back to cpi manually (if there were changes in iflow logic)
  10. Repeat 5-9

As a consequence of steps 6 and 9 we have to deal with "equivalence" of our local and remote iflows, and I'll cover that topic in next part.

Another Demo

I guess we'll close this one with another demo to see what it takes to setup a project from scratch (in previous video we had jars pre-downloaded)

00:00 Intro and quick Initial setup of capic

02:22 Start of download and some explanations

11:11 Some details about "git sync" and "blueprint editor" features

15:35 End of Download and final part

entry.title: Dynamic Interface on Cloud Integration - part 5
entry.content:
I’ll break this article into:

  • Problem/Context 
  • Our approach 
  • Git into Partner Directory 
  • Generic iflow 
  • Automated unit testing (this link)
  • Limitations and Future plans

One great advantage of relying on git is that you can have the freedom to organize your git repository however you want. We thought that we usually struggle on regular interface development to have sample data to test our interfaces. What if we could stored these test data on git as part of our scenario and unit test it at the end of the day?

This is exactly what we've done!

Let's create a folder named Sample at same level as the "Preparation Steps" folder.

Then inside of the Sample folder we'll have key pair files of REQ (requests) and their respective RSP (responses)

This is a great way to test for regressions since our Jenkins pipeline is prepared to check for each of the dynamic interfaces deployed if there is a 'Samples' folder. If so, it calls the dynamic interface with each of the REQ files and asserts a match on the response to the respective RSP associated file. Whenever running via test automation, we skip the sending to any target. It's just Unit testing of all payload transformations.

Now let's try to manually test one of these files just to see it working. We can test requirement 3 which was one of the most complex that we've defined. Example of input

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root>
	<process>CreateBatch</process>
	<region>EMEA</region>
	<prod_date>20240730</prod_date> <!-- format of dates in S4 is yyyymmdd -->
	<quantity>1000</quantity>
	<batch>1</batch>
	<batchplant>CH01</batchplant>
	<material>12345678901</material>
	<country>PT</country>
	<!-- other fields not relevant for this demo -->
</root>

 

What shall we expect?

It's a CreateBatch process so we shall format the date into yyyy-MM-dd, its EMEA region so the quantity shall be formatted. Finally country is PT therefore quantity shall be doubled. From target perspective, final url should be https://testnupeemeabatch.free.beeceptor.com

And it worked! 

In this chapter we covered how hard it is to find sample payload files for our tests and how cool would it be to test them automatically whenever they exist. On the final part we'll look into current limitations and future plans for this dynamic interface

entry.title: Content-Based or Non-Key Lookup in SAP Cloud Integration (SCI/CPI): Lookups Without Fixed Keys
entry.content:
Content-Based Lookup in SAP CPI Without Key-Value Pairs
In most SAP CPI integrations, we rely on key-based lookups using maps or content enrichers. However, there are scenarios where a key-value pair lookup is not feasible.

For instance, if the key involves an integer or double data type with a vast range, creating a mapping for every possible key-value pair becomes impractical. In such cases, we can use content-based search or iterative search to find the relevant matches dynamically.

Use Case: Matching a Person with a Group
Suppose we need to match a Person XML with a Group XML. A Person should be linked to a Group if their company, department, and quantity fields satisfy the given conditions. However, the quantity is a range of numbers, meaning that rather than looking for an exact match, we need to determine if the person's quantity falls within the specified range for a group. Once a match is found, the corresponding Group is attached to the Person Node.

My Approach:
Merge the Person and Group XML into a single structure using CPI functions. This allows us to process the data efficiently within a Groovy script.

Sample Input Payload:

 

<Messages>
  <Message1>
    <group>
      <group>
        <company>ABC</company>
        <department>MERCURY</department>
        <group>1</group>
        <contractMoreThan>0</contractMoreThan>
        <contractLessThan>5</contractLessThan>
      </group>
      <group>
        <company>DEF</company>
        <department>VENUS</department>
        <group>2</group>
        <contractMoreThan>5</contractMoreThan>
        <contractLessThan>11</contractLessThan>
      </group>
      <group>
        <company>DEF</company>
        <department>EARTH</department>
        <group>3</group>
        <contractMoreThan>10</contractMoreThan>
        <contractLessThan>20</contractLessThan>
      </group>
      <group>
        <company>DEF</company>
        <department>MARS</department>
        <group>3</group>
        <contractMoreThan>10</contractMoreThan>
        <contractLessThan>20</contractLessThan>
      </group>
      <group>
        <company>GHI</company>
        <department>JUPITER</department>
        <group>4</group>
        <contractMoreThan>30</contractMoreThan>
        <contractLessThan>365</contractLessThan>
      </group>
    </group>
    <Person>
      <Person>
        <id>111</id>
        <company>DEF</company>
        <department>EARTH</department>
        <quantity>13</quantity>
      </Person>
      <Person>
        <id>222</id>
        <company>GHI</company>
        <department>JUPITER</department>
        <quantity>350</quantity>
      </Person>
    </Person>
  </Message1>
</Messages>

 

 

 

 

 

Groovy Script for Matching
The following Groovy script processes the XML by iterating through each Person node and finding the corresponding Group based on company, department, and quantity conditions.

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.xml.XmlUtil
import groovy.util.Node
import groovy.util.slurpersupport.NodeChild

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String)
    def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)

    def root = new Node(null, 'root')

    xml.Message1.Person.Person.each { person ->
        def company = person.company.text()
        def department = person.department.text()
        def quantity = person.quantity.toDouble()

        println "company: $company"
        println "department: $department"
        println "quantity: $quantity"
        println "----"

        def personNode = new Node(null, 'Person')
        def clonedPerson = new Node(null, 'Person')

        person.children().each { child ->
            clonedPerson.append(new Node(null, child.name(), child.text()))
        }
        personNode.append(clonedPerson)

        def groups = xml.Message1.group.group
        def matchedGroup = groups.find { group ->
            def contractMoreThan = group.contractMoreThan.text().toDouble()
            def contractLessThan = group.contractLessThan.text().toDouble()
           
        ///**This is where we set the criteria for the match**//
            company == group.company.text() &&
            department == group.department.text() &&
            quantity > contractMoreThan &&
            quantity < contractLessThan
        }

        if (matchedGroup) {
            def matchedGroupNode = new Node(null, 'matchedGroup')
            matchedGroup.children().each { child ->
                matchedGroupNode.append(new Node(null, child.name(), child.text()))
            }
            personNode.append(matchedGroupNode)
        }

        root.append(personNode)
    }

    def outputXml = XmlUtil.serialize(root)
    message.setBody(outputXml)
    return message
}

 

 

 

 

Output: As you can see the script correctly matched the Group and Person

 

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root>
   <Person>
      <Person>
         <id>111</id>
         <company>DEF</company>
         <department>EARTH</department>
         <quantity>13</quantity>
      </Person>
      <matchedGroup>
         <company>DEF</company>
         <department>EARTH</department>
         <group>3</group>
         <contractMoreThan>10</contractMoreThan>
         <contractLessThan>20</contractLessThan>
      </matchedGroup>
   </Person>
   <Person>
      <Person>
         <id>222</id>
         <company>GHI</company>
         <department>JUPITER</department>
         <quantity>350</quantity>
      </Person>
      <matchedGroup>
         <company>GHI</company>
         <department>JUPITER</department>
         <group>4</group>
         <contractMoreThan>30</contractMoreThan>
         <contractLessThan>365</contractLessThan>
      </matchedGroup>
   </Person>
</root>

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

When a key-value pair lookup is not feasible, a content-based search approach like this is useful. By iterating through the XML and applying filtering logic dynamically, we can handle cases where an exact match is not predefined.

Please let me know if there are errors in this article or if there is anything we can further improve!

entry.title: Seamless Integration with SAP CI: Mastering Pagination for Coupa Adapter
entry.content:
Continuing my SAP CI Pagination blog series, this time with the Coupa Adapter! 

When integrating with Coupa, handling large datasets efficiently requires a proper pagination strategy. Unlike Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics CRM, which rely on next page links, Coupa uses an offset-based pagination approach. Here, data is fetched in chunks based on the offset and limit values set in the adapter.

In this blog, I’ll cover:
✅How pagination works in the Coupa adapter using offset & limit
✅Best practices for optimizing API calls and handling large datasets
✅A step-by-step implementation to ensure seamless data retrieval

If you missed the last blogs in this series on Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamic CRM adapters, below are the links:

Seamless Integration with SAP CI: Mastering Pagination for Salesforce Adapter

Seamless Integration with SAP CI: Mastering Pagination for Microsoft Dynamics CRM Adapter 

Coupa Adapter - Handling Large Data Sets with Pagination

In this use case, we focus on retrieving records from the invoices resource in Coupa, which contains 50+ records. To achieve this, we use Query Resource operation to retrieve data. While the adapter provides an Auto Pagination feature that retrieves all pages in a single call, this approach can lead to significant performance issues when dealing with large datasets.

Fetching data page by page, processing each batch, and then moving to the next page is the best approach for handling large datasets. In this blog, I will explain the end-to-end batching process, ensuring seamless data synchronization while optimizing API performance.

Design Approach: 

The scenario involves sending invoices resource from Coupa to a target system that has a batch limit of 10 records per request. To accommodate this constraint, we implement a custom pagination approach using Coupa receiver adapter call.

The call fetches the page of records within a looping process to retrieve the pages one after the other. Each fetched page is passed through message mapping, ensuring seamless transformation before being delivered to the target system. This cycle continues—fetching a page, processing it, and sending it to the target—until all records are transferred, ensuring an efficient full end to end Batching processing and controlled data flow from Coupa to the target system.

Design components elaboration:

Timer : This is a timer based flow which be a scheduled as per the user's input

Content Modifier : "set properties" - This step is used to declare propeties such as CoupaResource, fieldnames, Coupa_limit, Coupa_offset, StartDate.

Property NameUse
CoupaResourceName of the resource that has to be queried
fieldnamesfieldnames of the resource that has to be queried
Coupa_limitNumber of records per page
Coupa_offsetoffset value, is set as "0" as we intend to fetch all the pages without missing a single record
StartDateDate from which the records to be queried, refers to updated-at in Coupa resource

Understanding Offset and Limit in Coupa Pagination

In this design, the two key parameters to control data retrieval are Offset and Limit. Unlike cursor-based pagination (as seen in Salesforce and MS Dynamics), Coupa follows an offset-based pagination approach, where:

Limit – Defines the number of records per page (e.g., if set to 10, each API call fetches 10 records).
Offset – Determines the starting point for the next batch of records. In our design after each request, the offset is incremented by the limit value (10) to fetch the next set of data.

  • The initial request starts with Offset = 0 and Limit = 10.
  • The next request will have Offset = 10, Limit = 10, fetching records from 11 to 20.
  • This process continues in a loop until the last page is reached.

Looping Process Call : "Initiate Coupa Call"

This Looping Process Call continuously runs until the  field is absent from the response payload. The condition for looping is: ${property.RecordsPresent} = 'true'

I have used a Groovy script to help us determine when to stop fetching records.

🔹After each Coupa API call, the response is checked for the following error:

<errors>
   <error>No results match your search criteria.</error>
</errors>

🔹If this error is found, it means there are no more pages left, and the script sets:
 RecordsPresent = false (End of pagination)

🔹Otherwise, if valid records are present, the script sets:
RecordsPresent = true (Continue fetching the next page)

The Looping Process Call continues until RecordsPresent = 'false', ensuring controlled pagination.

Coupa receiver adapter:

Theadapter is placed inside the Looping Process Call to fetch records page by page.

Processing Tab

-- Operation : Query Resource

-- Resource : Resource dynamically passed from ${property.CoupaResource}

-- Return Object Fields : Fields dynamically passed from ${property.fieldnames}

-- Limit : Defines the number of records per page (e.g., 10 records per page). This value cannot be passed dynamically and only accepts numeric values. To control it, we declare "Coupa.limit" as a property.

-- Offset : Determines the starting point for data retrieval in pagination. It must always start at 0 and cannot be passed dynamically, as it only accepts numeric values. To handle this limitation, we declare "Coupa.offset" as a property and increment its value after every API call to fetch the next set of records.

TheAdapter supports setting this via the properties "Coupa.limit"and "Coupa.offset" allowing better control over pagination.

We are not digging into the connection tab in this blog, this blog focuses only on pagination concept.

Groovy Script : Set Coupa Offset Parameter

Below script control the offset incrementation as well as last page identification

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def processData(Message message) {
    // Get the property value from the message and cast it to an integer
    def propertyValueString = message.getProperty("Coupa_offset")
    def propertyValue = propertyValueString ? propertyValueString.toInteger() : 0 // Convert to integer, default to 0 if null or empty
    def Coupa_limit = message.getProperty("Coupa_limit") as Integer
    
    // Add 50 to the Coupa_offset
    propertyValue += Coupa_limit

    // Set the modified offset value back to the message
    message.setProperty("Coupa_offset", propertyValue.toString()) // Convert back to string for setting property

    // Parse the body of the message
    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    def slurper = new groovy.util.XmlSlurper().parseText(body)

    // Check if the specific error message is present in the body
    def errorPresent = slurper.depthFirst().find { it.name() == 'error' && it.text() == 'No results match your search criteria.' } != null

    // Set the CountObjects property based on the presence of the error message
    message.setProperty("RecordsPresent", !errorPresent)

    return message
}

 

Router : "Data?" -

A router is used to determine whether the fetched page contains any records. If records are present, the integration flow proceeds to process to next steps. However, if the page is empty, the flow terminates immediately, preventing unnecessary processing.

This check ensures optimized execution by stopping the pagination loop when there are no more records to fetch, improving efficiency and reducing unnecessary API calls.

Process Call : "Message Mapping" - 

After fetching each page, the data is passed to Message Mapping, where it is processed before being sent to the target system. This step is target-specific and is not covered in this blog.

For demonstration purposes, I have included this step to mimic the "send to target" operation after message mapping. The key focus of this blog remains on pagination handling in the Coupa adapter.

Output  / offset and limit explaintion:

For a total of 54 records, the pagination process spans 6 pages:

  • First 5 pages contain 10 records each.
  • Page 6 contains the remaining 4 records.

When the 7th page is fetched, the response contains as below, which means end of records/pages.

<errors> <error>No results match your search criteria.</error> </errors>

Page 1 : Response from the Coupa system contains 10 records as per the set limit and with offset = 0 

Page 2 : Offset = Offset (10)+ limit(10) = 20, the adapter skips the first 10 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 3 : Offset = Offset (20)+ limit(10) = 30, the adapter skips the first 20 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 4 : Offset = Offset (30)+ limit(10) = 40, the adapter skips the first 30 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 5 : Offset = Offset (40)+ limit(10) = 50, the adapter skips the first 40 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 6 : Offset = Offset (50)+ limit(10) = 60, the adapter skips the first 50 records and responds with next 10 records in this call. This is the last page containing 4 records.

Page 7 : Offset = Offset (60)+ limit(10) = 70, the adapter skips the first 50 records and responds with next 10 records in this call. <error> tag in the response which means end of records/pages

Full run in TRACE

Wrapping Up

With limit and offset handling in place, we achieve a structured and efficient pagination mechanism in Coupa Adapter. By leveraging properties like Coupa.limit and Coupa.offset, along with a controlled looping process, we ensure seamless data retrieval without unnecessary API calls.

This approach not only optimizes performance but also ensures that large datasets are processed efficiently and accurately. Stay tuned for the next blog in this series, where we continue exploring pagination strategies across different adapters! 

I hope this helps!

Cheers,

Punith

entry.title: Dynamic Interface on Cloud Integration - part 6
entry.content:
I’ll break this article into:

  • Problem/Context 
  • Our approach 
  • Git into Partner Directory 
  • Generic iflow 
  • Automated unit testing 
  • Limitations and Future plans (this link)

I hope our approach have given you some inspiration to try yourself to play around with the APIs and technology that is already there. In this final topic I would like to discuss some limitations and future goals for this interface. But first we start by what is supported already that was not discussed on this blog

  • Conversion from receiving format from JSON to XML: On many cases we realized we couldn't use the interface because we received JSON but the interface is ready just for XML, therefore we added a new parameter that once present will transform it as part of an initial step
  • Saving most parameters into custom headers: To make support life easier, we log as custom headers many of the parameters used so that they're searchable and easy to check
  • Adding static headers present on the parameter files into the dynamic: We found that not only the body but sometimes the headers need to be adapted depending on the rule, therefore we support defining different headers according to different configurations and we inject them into the process
  • Multiple http endpoints: We had one case where we had to distribute data into two or more different http urls sequentially so we incorporated that feature.
  • Multiple http adapter configurations: If you realize for HTTP adapter, you can't supply some parameters in a dynamic way (such as with SFTP), therefore we need to get all possible router for http to be able to respect whatever combination is supplied on the parameters (Proxy Type, Auth type, etc). If SAP once delivers this mechanism, we only need to change in this iflow and it will be something internal, so our expectation is that no process will be affected 

Limitations

Now we cover some limitations:

  • We support JSON as input translating into XML but we don't support converting from XML to JSON as output: We didn't have the need yet but an easy thing to incorporate
  • We don't support JSONPath or JMESPath equivalents to XPATH and JSONata or Jolt for JSON transformations: We believe they're not very known by most developers and we rather stick into XML 
  • All transformations we do are based on the current payload which might have been already transformed on previous steps of the rule execution. It can be that some of the key fields needed might not be there once you need them cause of a previous transformation: We can implement it whenever required to define on the lookup rules if the rule shall apply to the current or initially supplied XML
  • We don't support different user roles: So far only the standard ESBMessaging.send

Future plans

For future, I had an idea to implement some wizard that would create this parameters behind the scenes so that they can be prefilled in a non technical way by our business analysts.

Usually they know the formats (the source XML and the required target XML) and they even have some samples, so I developed a sample UI5 app (still in progress and almost fully generated by Chat GPT 😀), that on left side expects input files (REQ) and their respective output files (RSP). In the middle we have a chat that is determining an XSLT and executing it against all REQ and RSP files provided to assert it works for the supplied samples provided. In this case as it's in development you see the xslt in the middle doesn't comply with the files provided, therefore on the right side you see the red results.

In the end the business analysts could generate a proposal of these files into our integration team where we would just have to validate the configuration files provided and enhance them further if needed.

Of course, all this progress happens behind the scenes (mostly at weekends or at end of the day) therefore the timelines for such things will likely take time and would be experimental.

Anyway, please comment with other ideas, improvements, problems, I'll be happy to hear and improve the process on our side.

entry.title: Configure webhook notifications from SAP Cloud ALM to SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Webhook notifications allow SAP Cloud ALM (CALM) to automatically push updates to your designated endpoints whenever specific events occur. This eliminates the need for constant polling and ensures that you receive information instantly.

Example: whenever there is a change of status of a Task, CALM will trigger a webhook with notification along with basic info about the change into an iflow in SAP Cloud Integration (SCI)

Here we will set up notifications related to Tasks, but the same setup works for other resources like Requirements, Defects and so on. 
The configuration needs to be done at 4 different places:

  • Create webhook receiving iflow (SCI)
  • Set Endpoint in Landscape Management (CALM)
  • Create Webhook and Subscription in External API Management (CALM)
  • Assign Subscription to Project in Implementation (CALM)

Create webhook receiving iflow

Create a simple iflow with HTTPS Sender adapter, which will serve as the notification endpoint. Deploy it, so that the URL is available. It can be something like like https://[tenant_address]. hana.ondemand.com/http/tasks_notifications
Note down the path /http/tasks_notifications, or whatever you named it, as this we will use later.

Endpoint in Landscape Management

Navigate to Administration -> Landscape Management and select the system to which you want to send your notifications, in our case it will be SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration).
Go to Endpoint tab and create an endpoint with Use Case External API Management.

To be able to set the authentication, you need to create a Service Key in BTP Cockpit. Copy-paste the credential information into the Endpoint configuration and now you have a working connection to SCI.

Create Webhook and Subscription in External API Management

Navigate to Administration -> External API Management. On the left pane you can see Subscriptions management and Webhooks management. Go to Webhooks management, as we first create a Webhook and then assign it to Subscription.

Proceed with configuration as per the screenshot. LMS Service and Endpoint is what we set up in previous step. As Path set the path of our notification iflow from step 1. No need to specify the full URL, as that will be taken from the Endpoint.

Ext. Resource Type determines the type of resource we want to be notified about. Nevertheless, the same webhook can be used for other resources as well.

Now let's create Subscription. Just toggle onto Subscriptions management on the left pane, create a Subscription and assign the Webhook, which we created before. Again, follow the screenshot. 

Assign Subscription to Project in Implementation

Navigate to Implementation -> Projects and Setup and select your project.
It is important to point out that you can set up notifications per Project. You can be notified by changes on Tasks belonging into one Project, but Tasks of other Project will not trigger anything.

Go to Subscriptions tab and there you can activate notifications for individual Item Type. See that we can assign the same Subscription to all Item Types, regardless of what Resource Type we selected in previous step. 

Test our notifications

Now everything is set up. Go to any Task within the Project, for which we activated notifications and perform a change, e.g. switch it to another status.
Navigate to Administration -> External API Management and select Console from the left pane. Refresh if necessary and you will see all your webhook traffic.
If everything has been properly configured, you also received the payload into your SCI notification iflow.

entry.title: Automated Undeployment and deployment of selective iflows during planned downtime
entry.content:
During planned system downtime or in case of emergency downtime, we require all iflows to be undeployed at the start of downtime and then redeploy them again when the downtime is over. This task usually is performed manually and requires a dedicated person to be available to perform the activity. There are blogs created by fellow community members which provide solution to deploy-undeploy all iflows. However, there are cases where all iflows or artifacts need not be undeployed. For eg: in case of S4HANA downtime, only iflows inbound to S4HANA need to be undeployed while outbound ones, value mapping, script collections etc. do not require to be undeployed.

This blog provides detailed steps and components that are required to manage only selective iflows during downtime.

Un Deployment Process

The first step of setting up this functionality is to identify the list of iflows which are needed to be undeployed during outage. 

This list can be maintained in an excel file or notepad file with each Iflow ID in a separate line.

(i) Create a simple iflow to write the iflow list to a datastore. This entry in datastore should be a one-time activity unless there is a change in the list of iflows to be deployed or undeployed.

(ii) Un Deployment Process

Create an iflow to initiate the undeployment process.

 

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList) that was loaded in the first step.

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of undeployment.

Create an XSD like below which can be used for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use a csv to xml convert to convert the CSV payload into XML using the XSD created above.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the undeployment process.

 

Fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to undeploy the iflow

Check if the undeployment call was successful

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.  

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

Deployment Process

The deployment process is almost identical to the undeployment process except the endpoint and method that we use for deploying the iflow.

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList)

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of deployment.

Use the same xsd that we created in undeployment process for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the deployment process.

fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Set the response header set-cookie in an exchange property cookie.

set header cookie after formatting in groovy script as shown below

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to deploy the iflow

Check if the deployment is successful.

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

entry.title: Blog series on my CPI + Camel learning journey - Part2 - Camel in CPI
entry.content:
Link back to index: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/blog-series-on-my-cpi-camel-learning-journey/ba-p/14031053

Intro

First of all, Camel development to me is almost synonymous with Java development.
In a way, that it seems natural, that you have to be Java developer to do Camel properly. 
So keeping this in mind you might want to check proper official "Getting started guide" (and yes, camel app can be a real standalone Java application!)
 
But here we will first focus on abstractions Camel provides, and then try mapping it to CPI world (or the other way around) as we will do our best to keep away from any programming (including groovy) to follow the low-code paradigm.
 
Ideally, understanding of how "java-world-camel" really works under the hood could lead us (with help of our capic tool) to some insights of how we could achieve more in "low-code-world-cpi" (or maybe things we should avoid).

What do we have in Camel

So on this figure we see architecture overview
Not much to comment on, but that leaves us with following main abstractions:
  • CamelContext 
  • Route
  • Endpoint + Component
  • Processor
 
So far no Messages though!
Ok, lets try this one about CamelContext and see where we start having them maybe.
I strongly recommend thoroughly reading it through!
Here's the excerpt helping us to define entities we encountered earlier:
  1. The CamelContext is the runtime system, which holds together all the fundamental concepts of Apache Camel (Routes, Endpoints, Components, etc).
  2. Routes are a core abstraction for Camel. The simplest way to define a route is as a chain of Processors.
  3. To wire Processors and Endpoints together to form Routes, Camel defines a DSL.
  4. The Processor is a core Camel concept that represents a node capable of using, creating, or modifying an incoming Exchange.
 
Basically, it just so happened that CPI uses blueprint xml DSL to convert iflows into, thus giving us an option to play with all that stuff locally. But the "real one" is Java DSL of course.
 
And then there is really important section about Processors finally introducing Exchange and Message
There we read: "During routing, Exchanges flow from one processor to another; as such, you can think of a Route as a graph having specialized Processors as the nodes, and lines that connect the output of one Processor to the input of another."
 
And also: "At each Processor step, the output (Out) Message from the previous step is the input (In) Message of the next. In many cases, processors don’t set an Out Message, so in this case the In Message is reused."
 
Let's also have another figure from Red Hat Fuse guide also depicting Exchange flowing through the route.
There they define "Exchange object is a wrapper that encapsulates a received Message and stores its associated metadata (including the exchange properties). In addition, if the current Message is dispatched to a producer Endpoint, the Exchange provides a temporary slot to hold the reply (the Out Message)."
 
So now finally we see what's going on here: Exchange object is passed around, and Messages can be, well, exchanged by processors, so that next one receives what the previous returned (potentially NEW message).
 
To me it was confusing as in CPI they don't introduce us to this concept of "actual exchange" of messages (in plural), and this can mislead us to conceptualizing that both Exchange and Message are "singletons"
 
But here's some more info from this faq regarding In and Out Messages to clarify it finally:

  • The Out Message from each step is used as the In Message for the next step

  • if there is no Out Message then the In Message is used instead (!!!)

  • For the InOut MEP the Out from the last step in the route is returned to the producer. In case of InOnly the last Out is thrown away

Here InOut and InOnly MEP can be thought of as synchronous (sender waits for last Out message) and asynchronous communication. It is technically just a flag on Exchange though.
 
And finally, we might need something like this stolen from sap press blogs (who knows, maybe their book explains everything I am writing about in my blog series here) to illustrate Exchange to Messages relations.
 
So, key takeaways here are that Headers and Attachments are "attached" to a particular Message object (as of course Body is) that can be lost/exchanged, while Properties are on Exchange level.
Meaning, in theory, you can not rely on the fact that Headers and Attachments you add to your Message will "last" until the end of processing, while (exchange) Properties should.
Also don't forget that Out message (if processor returns it) becomes In one next step.
 

In short

So, to conclude this section let me try putting it all together in one sentence in a form of "The House That Jack Built":
  • Here's Message (having Body and Headers) we received through the Endpoint bound to some Component,
  • And transferred between chain of Processors (to be modified or exchanged) via and Exchange object (having it's own Properties)
  • Flowing through one or more Routes (defined in certain DSL) belonging to a certain CamelContext (sometimes separate)
  • Running in some OSGI runtime (thus "communicating" to other bundles) Apache built.
 
Basically, it means while there are some processors to process the exchange, camel route engine will pass it further down the graph.
The last processor to be called (not only within current route) is literally the end of the route processing.
 

What do we have in CPI

In CPI we have some other abstractions as iflows are modelled in BPMN notation.

So let's now try to take a look at CPI official documentation to find some hints at how it is mapped to Camel afterwards

 

Here's the quote:

"A graphical editor allows you, the integration developer, to model the message processing steps and specify in detail what happens to the message during processing.

In detail, you define the following aspects in an integration flow:

  • The senders and receivers of the message
  • How the senders and receivers are connected to the tenant (adapters)
  • The steps that define the message processing"
 
Ok.. not so informative, but at least this is something reminding us the Chain of Processors.
What if we now assume the following:
  • Adapters are camel Components (that are responsible for implementation of Endpoints logic)
  • Steps are camel Processors
  • Message is, well, camel Message (in most cases, In Message)
Next, we have another article in docs explaining "Camel Data Model"
 
Here we find that indeed "An integration flow is a BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation)-like model that allows you to specify how a message is to be processed on a tenant."
 
And also that "To interpret an integration flow model at runtime, it is transformed into an XML structure that is compatible with Apache Camel, an Open Source integration framework for Java that supports the mediation and routing of messages of any format."
 
What that really means is, our BPMN iflow becomes one or more camel routes within same camel context defined in blueprint xml dsl.
 
And yes, indeed they agree that we have Exchange object in place: "An Exchange can be seen as an abstraction of a message exchange process as it is executed by the Camel framework. An Exchange is identified uniquely by an Exchange ID. In the Properties area of the Exchange, additional data can be stored temporarily during message processing. This data is available for the runtime during the whole duration of the message exchange."
 

So what becomes what?

Let's now try to figure out how other elements of an iflow are mapped into camel model.
 
Well, it would be a good guess to think that Integration Process "shape" becomes a Route.
The same actually is correct for Local Integration Process.
But with Exception Subprocess it's not that easy - it actually becomes embedded in our route (we'll cover that later)
 
Another major difference would be that events (Start, End, Error, Escalation) also do NOT exist in Camel.
Though Start has at least semantics of from in camel world (as we connect our Sender adapter to it)
 
Other stuff would be more or less closely related to so-called EIPs
For example, Content Modifier would be a combination of setBody, setHeader, setProperty eips.
 
We're not aiming to cover it all here now (maybe later I'll do a separate post with some comparison table), but it is important to understand that on each step in iflow there is either a camel eip processor (eg router step becomes choice) or component/endpoint (eg <camel:toD uri="sap-map-pi..."/> for message mapping step).
// OK, sometimes like in case of HTTP receiver (ahc component, and I guess exactly because of Out Message existence) this becomes more complicated, so it is NOT a direct 1 to 1 mapping.
 
But what about receivers?
Well, again, no such thing in Camel, therefore we only have to (or toD) when we either define our Receiver as "final" step after "End Message" or when doing "Request Reply", or doing "Process Direct" and "Local Process" call - the only difference would be the Endpoint: direct (to route inside our Context), direct-vm (to route outisde our Context, but sap uses its own sap-processdirect) or some adapter like http or odata.
Anyway, in our route blueprint we will see something like <camel:to uri="..." />
 
This is VERY important to understand, and here's why.
As we said above, route is just a chain of processors we pass our exchange object to.
We only reach the end of that route when the last processor is called.
Now imagine we have an HTTP sender as we have in our simplest "Sender-initiated scenario with external data source and receiver"
 
It looks like this
HTTP adapter sets InOut MEP, which is not only synchronous in nature, but also means we shall respond with what the last message in chain is.
So, what would be return?
From what we see here it woud make sense to assume that we just return what our webshop odata call returns us, right? WRONG!
Why? Well, because we actually have something like at the end of this route:
<camel:to uri="sap-processdirect:foo?address=/custom/GenericReceiverDataStore"/>
So we kinda have something called "Message End", but does it stop camel from processing the exchange as usual? Of course, not!
Meaning, we actually will respond with what THIS guy will finish processing.
What I encourage you to do is to put Content Modifier where I have a red circle and set some random body.
And guess what? - you will see THAT body in your response from the Sender-Initiated scenario endpoint.
Again, this is exactly how Camel routing works, and this is exactly because of InOut (synchronous handling).
 

So what?

Well, I'd say, the biggest takeaway from this example is: in case you have InOut processing and don't change it to InOnly later (for example, placing messages into JMS queue, of which we have limited supply in CPI ironically), the whole processing is synchronous AND in-memory.
And that makes it oh-so-different from what you might read in this book where Message oriented architecture is Asynchronous (queue-oriented by nature).
This is where this BPMN low-code approach seems somewhat misleading to me in regards to EIP, ESB and all those enterprise level message oriented architectures: while it is possible to implement proper ESB on CPI, looks like it is NOT intended for really complex scenarions (eg cases like this with distributed transactions which camel supports while CPI does not even mention anywhere).
 

Moving on to Exceptions

As we said earlier, there is no such thing as Excepion Subprocess in camel, but there are onException clauses we can put to properly handle exceptions (who would expect that in java world)...
But then the question would be, how does End/Error/Escalation magic work?
Well, for those 3 examples from guidelines I made a new one almost identical to Sender-Initiated scenario we saw earlier.
It looks like this:
The main difference from this one is we don't use script step to "simulate exception" (why this even makes sense?..), we just instead rely on the fact that http adapter will throw one in case of 4xx/5xx answers.
In our case we will try reading Product by key, so it's trivial to trigger 404 here (which is also a rather realistic scenario, I'd say).
So, is there a way to see what does the camel route look like?
Yes, there is, and in our demo for this part I'll show it in action. Here are the screenshots of camel context outline feature we have in capic to quickly check routes and from/to uris we have.
So, here's how our End, Error, and Escalation events look like (respectively):

In all cases we see that "success path" goes to our Receiver, while inside onException things start looking weird.

Whats going on there?

Well, our outline only shows us part of the picture of course, but at least we can see that Escalation case is different as message is sent to sap-alert (which explains why you have some generic error in response).

How are End and Error cases are different though?

Well, with this another tool will gladly help us - git!

First picture is a diff between End and Error, while the second one - between Error and Escalation

Here we see <camel:handled> changed from true to false and also that sap-specific exchange property 

"SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed"=false is removed (meaning it is true by default)
Here we see that  <camel:handled> changed back from false to true and that <camel:to uri="sap-alert://escalation..."/> receiver showed up.

Basically, thats it: <camel:handled> just signals to Camel itself if route was able to handle the exception (and in the demo I'll show what happens when our local ftp sender case fails to do so), while some custom exchange property ("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed") or any additional receiver ("sap-alert://escalation") does not make any difference  to Camel runtime.

And hence, this is how our local blueprint outline would look like:

In both cases we send message to our Generic Receiver, and the only difference is if we mark <camel:handled> as true or not.

Next Demo

Basically, we just modify our Exception Subprocess scenarion on CPI side, then sync it to local git and make local blueprints to compare them to each other.

It ended up to be a pretty long one though...

00:00 Intro and quick initial setup

03:28 Rezip bundles and test sample iflow (remote and local)

09:03 Sync iflow from CPI to git and make new local blueprint for End message case

21:07 Change iflow to Error event and sync and make local blueprint again

31:22 Change iflow to Escalation event and sync and make local blueprint again

40:13 (Unplanned) Revert iflow to End message and make a final conclusion regarding local blueprints equivalence

entry.title: Machine Translation in the Documentation for SAP Integration Suite & SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello, integration community!

Exciting news just dropped — machine translation is now available for the documentation of SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Integration on SAP Help Portal! This feature brings us one step closer to giving you seamless access to all the resources you need, no matter what language you speak.

Why This Matters

If you’ve ever found yourself navigating documentation in a language you’re not entirely comfortable with, you know what a game-changer this is. With machine translation at your fingertips, you can easily understand complex instructions, learn best practices, and troubleshoot issues without any linguistic hurdles. Whether you're trying to integrate new systems, troubleshoot an API issue, or simply understand the latest features, having documentation in your preferred language can make all the difference.

How It Works

Wondering how this new feature works? It’s simple! The machine translation tool harnesses cutting-edge technology to provide real-time translations of our docs into multiple languages. Sure, no machine translation can be perfect, but we’re confident it’ll make your life easier.

You can try it right now: Go to the documentation for SAP Integration Suite or SAP Cloud Integration and next to the title, choose your preferred language from a list of additional languages.

Your Feedback Matters

We understand that machine translations might not always be flawless. That’s why we’ve made it easy for you to provide feedback: If you spot an error or feel a translation could be improved, let us know in our survey. You can find it in the disclaimer at the top of the page or the side bar in the Help Portal. Your input will help us refine the machine translation and ensure it meets everyone's needs.

We’re thrilled to see how machine translation can enhance your experience with SAP Integration Suite and SAP Cloud Integration. So, go ahead, give it a try, and don’t hesitate to share your thoughts with us!

entry.title: Integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post provides a step-by-step guide on integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the SAP Integration Suite. The focus is on setting up a straightforward integration flow (iFlow) to extract contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2 and create corresponding contacts in SAP Emarsys.

The goal is to demonstrate how to connect both systems, using APIs and middleware to automate data transfer. This serves as a foundation for more complex integrations in the future, making it easier to extend the process with additional functionality. A key aspect of this integration is OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, which is required to securely access SAP Emarsys. We will cover how authentication works in this context and how to configure it properly within the iFlow.

 

Prerequisites

Before implementing this iFlow ensure that you have the following:

  • An active SAP Integration Suite tenant with Cloud Integration enabled
  • Access to a SAP Sales Cloud V2 account with API permissions
  • Access to an SAP Emarsys account with API permissions

 

Implementation

Overview of the Integration Flow

Overview of the iFlow between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using SAP Integration SuiteThe diagram above provides an overview of the iFlow, which connects SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. This process automates retrieving contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, processes the information, and transfers it to SAP Emarsys to create corresponding contacts.

  1. Start Timer – Initiates the iFlow execution at predefined intervals.
  2. Set Filter – Applies conditions to select only relevant contacts from SAP Sales Cloud V2.
  3. Get Sales Cloud Contacts – Fetches contact data via the SAP Sales Cloud API.
  4. Extract Sales Cloud Contacts – Processes and structures the extracted data.
  5. Configure SAP Emarsys Request –Prepares the Request and Setting Up OAuth Authentication
  6. Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys – Sends the contact data via an API request to SAP Emarsys.

In the following sections, each step of the iFlow will be explained in detail.

Step 1: Start Timer - Triggering the Integration Flow

Timer Configuration – Scheduling the iFlow to run at regular intervals

The iFlow is initiated by a timer-based trigger, which ensures that contact data is regularly synchronized between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys.

As shown in the screenshot, the timer is configured using a Simple Schedule, where the iFlow runs every hour between February 27, 2025, at 09:00 UTC and March 6, 2025, at 18:00 UTC.

Step 2: Set Filter - Filtering Relevant Contacts

Configure SAP Sales Cloud V2 Request

In this step, we apply a filter to ensure that only the necessary contacts are fetched from SAP Sales Cloud V2. First, we limit the selection to contacts associated with the United States. Second, we include only those that have been newly created or updated within the last hour.

After adding the timer to trigger the iFlow, the next step is to insert a Content Modifier to define the query parameters for retrieving the relevant contact data.

  1. Add a Content Modifier
  2. Navigate to the Message Header tab.
  3. Create a new header entry:
    • Action: Create
    • Name: CamelHttpQuery (you can choose your own name)
    • Source Type: Expression
    • Source Value: $filter=isContactPersonFor/country eq 'US' and (adminData/createdOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z' or adminData/updatedOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z'})

The filter dynamically adjusts the timestamp to the beginning of the current hour, ensuring that each execution of the integration flow retrieves only the latest contact records.

Step 3: Get Sales Cloud Contacts - Fetching Data from SAP Sales Cloud V2

To retrieve contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, we need to set up a Request Reply and connect it to an HTTP Receiver Adapter. This ensures that the iFlow sends a request to SAP Sales Cloud V2 and processes the response dynamically.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

The Request Reply step is required to handle synchronous communication with SAP Sales Cloud. It ensures that the request is sent, and the response is processed before moving to the next step in the iFlow.

  1. Add a Request Reply step after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply step to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

The HTTP Receiver Adapter is responsible for sending the request to SAP Sales Cloud V2. The configuration is as follows:

Configure the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2

  • Address: The endpoint for retrieving contact persons:
